data.title: Apple Says Its Ads and Maps Services Meet EU Digital Markets Act Thresholds
data.description:
Apple (AAPL, Financials) told the European Commission that two of its core platform services, Apple Ads and Apple Maps, fall within the thresholds defined under the EU's Digital Markets Act, according to a statement released Friday. The disclosure triggers a formal review period during which regulators will decide whether either service should be designated as a gatekeeper under the law.

The Commission now has 45 working days to issue its decision. If Apple is designated, the company will have six months to meet the DMA's requirements, which focus on ensuring fair competition and limiting practices that could restrict user choice. Apple has faced increasing regulatory attention in Europe as lawmakers broaden enforcement of the bloc's competition and digital-services frameworks.

The company did not publicly comment on the filing beyond the Commission's statement. The DMA review process adds another layer of regulatory oversight to Apple's operations in the region, where the company's app distribution, payment systems and advertising tools have been under scrutiny.

Investors will monitor whether any eventual designation leads to changes in how Apple integrates these services across its ecosystem.

data.title: Oracle Draws Bank Interest for $38 Billion in New Funding to Support OpenAI Sites
data.description:
Oracle (ORCL, Financials) is holding preliminary discussions with several banks about a loan package of roughly $38 billion to help build more data-center sites for OpenAI, according to a Financial Times report citing people familiar with the matter. Reuters has not confirmed the details, but the talks reflect how quickly capital requirements are rising across the AI infrastructure sector.

The potential financing would back projects developed with Vantage Data Centers, a partner in earlier efforts to expand capacity for OpenAI workloads. Banks have shown continued interest in supporting these buildouts, even as the size of the commitments grows. Oracle has already raised significant debt this year, including an $18 billion bond sale and multibillion-dollar project loans tied to planned facilities in New Mexico, Texas and Wisconsin.

If the new package moves forward, it would rank among the largest AI-related financings so far. Investors will look to Oracle's Dec. 15 earnings call for guidance on how the company intends to manage the funding needs associated with its expanding data-center pipeline and long-term AI strategy.

data.title: Intel Executives Face Scrutiny as Taiwan Raids Former TSMC Leader's Homes in Trade-Secrets Probe
data.description:
Intel (INTC, Financials) is under renewed scrutiny after Taiwan prosecutors searched the residences of former TSMC senior vice president Wei-Jen Lo as part of an investigation into alleged trade-secret leaks. Authorities said investigators executed warrants on Wednesday, removing computers, USB drives and other items while also securing court approval to seize Lo's shares and real estate.

TSMC, the world's largest contract chipmaker and a major supplier to companies including Nvidia, filed a lawsuit this week accusing Lo of a high probability of using or disclosing confidential information to Intel, where he began working in October after a 21-year tenure at TSMC. Prosecutors said Lo is suspected of violating Taiwan's National Security Act, which governs the protection of core technologies.

Intel rejected the allegations earlier on Thursday, saying it has no reason to believe Lo engaged in improper conduct. The company said its policies prohibit the transfer of third-party intellectual property and described Lo as a respected industry leader. Lo, who previously spent 18 years at Intel before joining TSMC in 2004, did not respond to requests for comment.

data.title: Intel--TSMC Trade?Secret Bombshell Erupts Just as Nvidia Earnings Hit the Spotlight
data.description:
Investors watching Intel (INTC, Financial) this week are getting a plot twist just as the entire semiconductor space positions for Nvidia's earnings. Intel pushed back against allegations from Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM, Financial) that former senior vice president Wei-Jen Lo may have leaked confidential information after joining the U.S. chipmaker in October. In an emailed statement, Intel said it has no reason to believe the claims have merit and reiterated that its policies prohibit the transfer or use of any third-party intellectual property. The company also described Lo—who returned after an earlier 18-year stint at Intel and a 21-year run at TSMC—as a respected industry figure with a long track record of technical leadership.

TSMC has taken a different view, filing a lawsuit in Taiwan's Intellectual Property and Commercial Court and arguing there is a high probability Lo could have used, disclosed or transferred its trade secrets. Taiwan's economy ministry signaled it will cooperate on determining whether the case touches on core technologies or possible National Security Act violations. Lo did not respond to comment requests, and Intel framed the situation as part of normal talent movement in a globally competitive industry, though the timing could be raising eyebrows across the market.

All of this lands as AMD (AMD, Financial), Intel and TSMC brace for Nvidia's (NVDA, Financial) results—an event that could be setting the tone for how investors position around advanced manufacturing, supply visibility and sector leadership. With Lo having contributed to TSMC's push into 5-nm, 3-nm and 2-nm mass production, the dispute introduces an added layer of uncertainty at a moment when traders are already watching every signal from Nvidia for clues on demand trajectory and competitive intensity.

data.title: ASML Stock Climbs After Morgan Stanley Tags It a 'Top Pick' for 2026
data.description:
ASML (ASML, Financial) shares climbed about 4% on Wednesday U.S trading after Morgan Stanley raised its price target to €1,000 from €975 and named Dutch chip-equipment maker its "Top Pick" in European semiconductors.

The bank says demand is firming across memory and logic as DRAM producers shift to newer nodes, which should increase lithography layers and drive EUV intensity into fiscal 2026. Analyst Lee Simpson cited the lift from DRAM transitions and said higher EUV sales alongside a richer product mix may help sustain margins.

Morgan Stanley models a 52.3% gross margin for 2026, about 40 basis points below last year, and expects China demand to fall roughly 15%, less severe than prior company guidance. The team also flagged upside risks from DRAM and foundry spending tied to AI infrastructure and said recent share weakness offers an "attractive entry point."

The bank reiterated an Overweight rating and noted Nvidia (NVDA, Financial)'s robust Blackwell demand as a positive read-through for ASML's tool cycles. Investors will watch order visibility and momentum from Samsung and SK Hynix as the key near-term catalyst.

data.title:
Palantir: The Most Expensive Stock in History Has Big Problems
        (Status: Pending)
data.description:
Palantir Technologies (PLTR, Financial) faces fresh scrutiny as investors digest a mix of strong sales and heavy insider selling. Although the company posted solid growth, revenue jumped about 63% to roughly $1.2 billion in the most recent quarter, yet the stock trades with an unusually rich valuation for a company of its size.

CEO Alex Karp publicly criticized short sellers on CNBC after the earnings release, which drew attention because he and other executives have been filing plans to sell shares. In early November, Karp filed to sell roughly 585,000 shares at about $163.99 per share, part of a broader pattern of sales that topped $200 million in recent filings. Executives say many transactions run through prearranged 10b5-1 plans, used for diversification and tax planning, but investors naturally weigh those sales against public rhetoric.

Market metrics raise questions. With roughly 2.57 billion shares outstanding and a market cap that exceeded $500 billion at recent peaks, Palantir traded at very high multiples compared with traditional peers. Analysts and data providers point out forward multiples that require sustained above-average growth to justify current prices.

Competition adds another layer. Snowflake (SNOW, Financial) and other data and AI firms have shown that market sentiment can shift quickly, and private players such as Databricks keep the competitive landscape dynamic.

Palantir is continuing to lose a fast-expanding business that has strong government and corporate integrations. However, when you consider investing, you must be on the lookout for insider selling, stock-based compensation that waters the shareholders down, and the likelihood of the current projected growth exceeding the already sky-high expectations already embedded in the shares.

In the meantime, the company's performance and leadership position put it at a stock worth maintaining a watch in lieu of merely taking part in the hype.

data.title: Cathie Wood Buying Dip in CoreWeave After 45% Crash -- Should You?
data.description:
Ark Invest boosted its exposure to CoreWeave (CRWV, Financial) on Monday, stepping in after the AI-infrastructure provider's shares retreated sharply over the past month.

The bulk of the buying occurred in the ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK, Financial), which added 396,178 shares. Following the move, ARKK's stake is valued at roughly $52.4 million, representing about 0.7% of the fund.

The ARK Next Generation Internet ETF (ARKW, Financial) increased its position as well, acquiring 41,167 shares. ARKW now holds approximately $35.8 million in CoreWeave, making the stock its 19th-largest weight at about 1.7% of assets.The investment firm purchased a combined 437,345 CoreWeave (CRWV, Financial) shares across two of its active ETFs.

CoreWeave has lost close to 45% over the last month amid cooling sentiment toward high-growth AI names. Ark has historically used sharp drawdowns to accumulate positions it views as tied to long-term technology themes, including advanced compute infrastructure and cloud-based acceleration services.

Is CRWV Stock a Buy?

Based on the one year price targets offered by 26 analysts, the average target price for CoreWeave Inc is $131.80 with a high estimate of $234.00 and a low estimate of $36.00. The average target implies a upside of +84.87% from the current price of $71.29.

Based on the consensus recommendation from 31 brokerage firms, CoreWeave Inc's (CRWV, Financial) average brokerage recommendation is currently 2.5, indicating a "Outperform" status. The rating scale ranges from 1 to 5, where 1 signifies Strong Buy, and 5 denotes Sell.

data.title: Tesla Europe Sales Crash 48% -- BYD Triples Sales in Aggressive Takeover
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA, Financial) Europe registrations nearly halved in October, falling 48.5% to 6,964 units.

In contrast, Chinese rival BYD (1211) sold 17,470 cars in the region in October, up about 207% year-on-year, lifting its market share to roughly 1.6% while Tesla's share slipped to 0.6%.

Overall passenger car registrations rose 4.9% to about 1.09 million units, with hybrid electric vehicles remaining the largest segment.

Market observers say BYD's broader model range, including plug-in hybrids, has helped it sidestep some tariff pressure and win buyers across Europe.

Analysts add Tesla's weak European momentum reflects tougher competition and consumer headwinds tied to CEO Elon Musk's public profile.

The outcome demonstrates the potential of the rapid EV market in Europe to turn within a short time. Dealers and investors will continue monitoring the question of whether the BYD boom will continue to last to Q4 and whether Tesla will be capable of stabilizing demand with new product variations.

Regulators will watch production plans and tariffs as local manufacturing and pricing strategies could reshape European EV competition next year.
